name: Red Velvet Cake with History
recipeIngredient: 2 teaspoons to 1 ounce of red food color
recipeInstructions:
Red velvet cake, also known as the $100 dollar cake, $200 dollar cake, or Waldorph Astoria Cake, is a legendary dessert that challenges consumers who feel they have been overcharged. The recipe is a grassroots one, not an institutional one.
James Beard outlines in his American Cookery that there are three varieties of red velvet cake: recipes using butter, vegetable shortening (crisco), and oil. Most recipes call for cake flour and buttermilk, baking soda, vinegar, and cocoa, which cause a reddish-brown color due to the chemical reaction between these ingredients.
The redness comes from red food coloring despite the chemical reaction of baking soda, vinegar, buttermilk, and cocoa. The cake is moist and has a velvety texture with a nondistinctive flavor, as is typical for most red velvet cakes.
Red Velvet cake is also cultural, as recipes differ in community cookbooks across America, even in terms of the type of frosting used. The moistest cake is made with oil and cake flour. Start with 2 teaspoons of food color as a base; adjust to taste.
Preheat oven to 350 degrees Fahrenheit. Grease and flour two 9-inch pans and line with paper. In a large bowl, combine flour, baking soda, cocoa, and salt. In another bowl, beat sugar and oil until blended. Add eggs one at a time, blending well after each addition. Blend in food color, vanilla, and vinegar.
Alternately blend in the flour mixture and buttermilk, using about 1/3 of each batch and scraping the bowl down at least twice. Do not beat on high; the cake will be tough if overmixed. Pour into prepared pans and bake for 20 to 25 minutes or until a toothpick inserted comes out clean. Cool on racks for 5 minutes before removing from pan and allowing to cool completely.
Frost layers when cold. Beat cream cheese until softened and smooth. Add butter and continue to beat until softened. Beat in sugar a little at a time, then vanilla. If the frosting is too thick, blend in cold milk a tablespoon at a time until desired consistency is achieved.
